title: Combining Sources in Series
learningObjectives: Combine two DC sources in series to raise total supply voltage
Predict how changing source voltage affects circuit current
Use resistance to keep current and power within safe limits
introText: Stacking battery packs in series raises the available voltage. In this lesson you'll connect two sources, observe the higher supply, and tame the current with a larger resistor.

steps.instructionText: Place two DC Voltage Sources and a resistor, then wire them in a single closed loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Nice wiring! Everything is connected so current can flow through both sources and the resistor.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Make sure both sources and the resistor are wired together. Every component needs a wire on each side.

steps.instructionText: Set each source to about 6V so the total supply is roughly 12V.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Great — two 6V sources in series add up to about 12V across the loop.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Adjust the voltage sliders on both sources until the total voltage reads close to 12V.

steps.instructionText: Increase the resistor to around 600Ω to keep current under 25 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is safely below 25 mA. More resistance keeps the higher voltage from pushing too much current.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor value. Higher resistance reduces the series current even with 12V applied.

questions.questionText: Two 6V sources are placed in series with a 600Ω resistor. What total voltage does the circuit see?
questions.explanation: Voltages in series add: 6V + 6V = 12V supplied to the loop.

questions.questionText: With 12V across a 600Ω resistor, what is the current?
